summary: The main claim is: So, they have the sense, 'Oh, we've been writing up these concerns for 20 years and no one listens to us.' My view is quite different.
...So, when it comes to AGI and existential risk, it turns out as best I can ascertain, in the 20 years or so we've been talking about this seriously, there isn't a single model done .
The main claim is: Here's the link: The issue seems to be that ChatGTP made up a news story and attributed it to a journalist who worked for The Guardian.
I'm not quite sure how the researcher who was using ChatGTP was posing questions but suspect that might have something to do with the outcome.
This post is a transcript of a recorded discussion, so it doesn't argue a single main claim — open the original to read the conversation.
The main claim is: We shouldn’t think of something called “interpretability” as being fundamentally separate from other tools that can help us accomplish our goals with models.

top_comment.author: DanielFilan
top_comment.text: Re: the gorilla example, seems worth noting that the solution that was actually deployed ended up being refusing to classify anything as a gorilla, at least as of 2018 (perhaps things have changed since then).
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: Re: the gorilla example, seems worth noting that the solution that was actually deployed ended up being refusing to classify anything as a gorilla, at least as of 2018 (perhaps things have changed since then).

summary: The main claim is: One potential solution would be to train the AI in a simulated world, where it is led to believe that it is human and must contend with the same needs and emotions as we do.
While I recognize that we currently do not have the technology to create such a "training wheel" system, I believe it may be the best way to filter out potentially destructive AI.
The main claim is: I think this will confuse many people because of how central AI alignment is to the shard theory project.
I think precisifying and clarifying that will be an important part of building shard theory.
The main claim is: Because if we picked a random utility function, we get a safely random output.
So all humans need to do is be better than random in our choice of utility function.
The main claim is: Crossposted from the EA Forum: Epistemic status: quick and dirty reaction to the claim that alignment research is like archaeology.
This can only mean that either alignment is not at a pre-paradigmatic state or that archaeology is not a mature science.

The main claim is: I'll outline a few reasons I think this is an undesirable state of affairs, but on a surface level I feel it should be obvious to most people convinced of the severity of the issue why the alignment problem should be garnering at least as…

I don't expect a project like this to bring AI safety into the mainstream, but I feel like it would be a massive help in allowing someone worried about the issue to express to someone unfamiliar with the problem why they're concerned about…
The main claim is: This competition is for short-form arguments for the importance of AI safety.
They may want to know how the arguments could affect what they should be researching.
The main claim is: It is, to the best of our knowledge, the largest dense autoregressive model that has publicly available weights.
GPT-NeoX-20B is a 20 billion parameter autoregressive language model whose weights will be made freely and openly available to the public through a permissive license.
The main claim is: So S1 has value of information, response incentive, and value of control, but not an instrumental control incentive.
Value of control : what does the agent want to control?
The main claim is: Even if the principals agree on a welfare function, it is possible that many different profiles of agents optimize the same welfare function.
The payoffs to a principal in this game depend on how the agents behave over time.
